title: Going Out & Plans
titleFr: Les sorties et le futur proche
cefrDescriptors: Can talk about near-future plans and arrangements.
Can use the futur proche (aller + infinitive).
concept:
Good news: you can talk about the future without learning a whole new tense yet. French has a shortcut called the futur proche ("near future"), and it's built from a verb you'll use everywhere — aller (to go).

First, learn aller in the present:
  je vais, tu vas, il/elle va,
  nous allons, vous allez, ils/elles vont.

The recipe for the near future is simple: aller (conjugated) + a verb in the infinitive (its base form). That's it.
  je vais manger (I'm going to eat)
  on va aller au cinéma (we're going to go to the cinema)
  tu vas sortir ? (are you going to go out?)
It works for any verb: nous allons regarder un film, ils vont travailler.

Notice « on va aller » — yes, aller can appear twice (the helper aller + the main verb aller, "going to go"). That's normal.

Pair it with time markers that point forward: demain (tomorrow), ce soir (tonight), ce week-end (this weekend). And the social glue of making plans: inviter (to invite), sortir (to go out), avec (with), ensemble (together), peut-être (maybe), and the cheerful « On y va ! » (Let's go!).

Now you can make plans: « Ce week-end, je vais sortir avec des amis. On va aller au cinéma. » Write two real plans you have, using aller + an infinitive each.

vocabulary.exampleFr: Ce week-end, je vais à Paris.
vocabulary.exampleEn: This weekend, I'm going to Paris.
We're going to watch a film.

vocabulary.exampleFr: Il y a une fête ce soir.

examples.en: I'm going to go to the cinema.

examples.fr: Ce soir, on va manger au restaurant.
examples.en: Tonight, we're going to eat at the restaurant.

examples.fr: Nous allons regarder un film ensemble.
examples.en: We're going to watch a film together.
